provenance: Synthesized from 'Implementation-Ready Microdrama Scriptwriting and Evaluation Framework' (input-research/, pages 25-30) under the 8 founder rulings of 2 Jul 2026. Every deviation is documented in SYNTHESIS_NOTES.md. Anchor levels 1/3/5/7/10 carry the source's verbatim keyword; levels 2/4/6/8/9 are interpolated and flagged _derived.

parameters.name: Opening hook strength
parameters.definition: Whether the opener creates immediate compulsion: the first beat changes status, knowledge, or threat.

1.text: Flat - the opener creates no compulsion; nothing changes status, knowledge, or threat.

2.text: Nearly flat - a faint stir of tension, but the first beat is still setup and demands nothing.

3.text: Readable but weak - the opening's intent is legible, yet the first beat lands softly and generates little pull.

4.text: Approaching functional - a real hook exists but arrives late or undersold.

5.text: Functional - a clear hook exists and lands on time, but it is ordinary; it holds rather than grabs.

6.text: Above functional - the hook is clear and has one distinct element (image, line, or accusation) that raises curiosity.

7.text: Strong - the first beat immediately changes status, knowledge, or threat in a way that demands the next beat.

8.text: Very strong - immediate, specific, and fresh; the hook creates a question a viewer will not abandon.

9.text: Near-irresistible - a first beat that would stop a scroll cold; only marginal polish separates it from best-in-class.

10.text: Irresistible and clean - an instantly legible, compulsive opening (accusation, danger, public exposure) with zero wasted setup.

parameters.name: Protagonist clarity and emotional alignment
parameters.definition: Whether the viewer knows whose pain and want matter: clear goal, wound, and identification.

1.text: No anchor - the viewer cannot tell whose story this is or why to care.

2.text: Barely anchored - a lead is identifiable but their want and wound are absent.

3.text: Anchor vague - the lead is visible but goal or injury is fuzzy; competing focal points blur alignment.

4.text: Emerging - goal or wound is stated but not yet felt; alignment flickers.

5.text: Decent - the lead's goal and injury are readable; the viewer aligns, without strong pull.

6.text: Solid - goal, wound, and desire are all present and consistent across the episode.

7.text: Strong - a specific, personal want and injury make the viewer feel the lead's stakes as their own.

8.text: Very strong - the lead's pain and want drive every beat; alignment never breaks.

9.text: Near-vivid - deeply personal and specific; a single beat short of unforgettable.

10.text: Vivid and personal - the lead's desire and injury are so specific and personal the audience cannot help but take their side.

parameters.name: Conflict and stakes density
parameters.definition: How much active pressure exists: obstacles, countdowns, shame, loss, danger.

1.text: Inert - no active opposition or cost anywhere; people talk, nothing presses.

2.text: Nearly inert - one mild obstacle exists but carries no consequence.

3.text: Thin - pressure appears in patches; long stretches carry no risk of loss.

4.text: Sparse - real stakes exist but the pressure releases between beats.

5.text: Adequate - consistent conflict with visible cost, though the pressure is familiar and evenly paced.

6.text: Firm - conflict compounds; at least one countdown, shame, or danger tightens mid-episode.

7.text: Pressurized - overlapping obstacles and social cost keep every scene under load.

8.text: Heavily pressurized - escalating, layered stakes; each beat raises what can be lost.

9.text: Near-relentless - pressure never releases and keeps sharpening; marginal slack remains in one beat at most.

10.text: Relentless - every beat applies new, meaningful pressure; obstacles, countdowns, and social cost interlock without repetition.

parameters.name: Episode structure and pacing
parameters.definition: Beat efficiency and escalation: late entry, no drift, rising pressure across the micro-slot.

1.text: Shapeless - no discernible beat structure; scenes wander without escalation.

2.text: Barely shaped - a structure is intended but buried under setup and drift.

3.text: Saggy - the spine exists but the middle drifts; entries are early and exits late.

4.text: Loose - beats land in order but at TV pace, too slow for the micro-slot.

5.text: Competent - clean beat order and functional pacing; escalation present but even.

6.text: Tight-ish - late entries and early exits in most scenes; only one beat overstays.

7.text: Crisp - every beat enters late, exits early, and escalates; no drift anywhere.

8.text: Very crisp - compression creates momentum; structure itself generates tension.

9.text: Near-perfect economy - every second earns its place; one merge away from optimal.

10.text: Perfectly economical - not a wasted beat; setup is folded into pressure and the escalation curve never flattens.

parameters.name: Twist and reveal effectiveness
parameters.definition: Quality of reversals and knowledge shifts: seeded turns that change power.

1.text: Absent or cheap - no reversal, or a shock with no seeding and no consequence.

2.text: Token - a turn exists but is arbitrary; nothing changes because of it.

3.text: Predictable - the reveal is visible long before it lands; power barely shifts.

4.text: Serviceable-minus - the turn lands but its seeding is thin or its consequence delayed.

5.text: Works - a legible, seeded reveal that shifts knowledge, executed conventionally.

6.text: Good - seeded and consequential; the power shift is felt immediately.

7.text: Strong - a well-seeded reversal that meaningfully rearranges power and raises the next question.

8.text: Very strong - surprising yet inevitable in hindsight; consequences ripple through the episode.

9.text: Near-exceptional - shocking and fully earned; a hair short of best-in-class execution.

10.text: Shocking yet earned - a reveal the audience never saw coming that was fairly seeded all along, with immediate, heavy consequence.

parameters.name: Cliffhanger and next-episode pull
parameters.definition: Force of the continuation cue: a precise unanswered debt at the episode's end.

1.text: None - the episode simply stops or resolves; no unanswered question remains.

2.text: Trace - a loose thread exists but no one would notice it as a cue to continue.

3.text: Soft - a vague mood ending; unease without a precise question.

4.text: Mild - a question exists but is small or answerable by assumption.

5.text: Serviceable - a clear unanswered question that would carry a patient viewer forward.

6.text: Firm - a specific debt (threat, secret, decision) left open at the cut.

7.text: Strong - a precise, actionable uncertainty that makes skipping the next episode feel costly.

8.text: Very strong - the cut lands mid-consequence; the question is urgent and personal.

9.text: Near-compulsive - an ending engineered for the immediate next tap; a frame's polish from perfect.

10.text: Immediate tap desire - an ending that converts directly into the next episode: one precise, unbearable question at maximum pressure.

parameters.name: Dialogue economy and performability
parameters.definition: Whether lines do clean story work: short, specific, actable, subtitle-friendly.

1.text: Bloated - long, explanatory speeches; lines restate emotion instead of acting.

2.text: Heavy - exposition dominates; a few functional lines survive.

3.text: Generic - lines are short enough but interchangeable; no voice, no status play.

4.text: Passable - mostly functional lines with recurring generic patches.

5.text: Fine - economical and performable; does the work without distinction.

6.text: Good - compressed, specific lines; status play visible in exchanges.

7.text: Sharp - every line attacks, defends, reveals, or decides; subtitle-friendly and actable.

8.text: Very sharp - compression creates subtext; lines are quotable in places.

9.text: Near-exceptional - emotionally specific, highly compressed; a line or two from best-in-class.

10.text: Quotable and lean - maximum story work per word; lines survive dubbing and subtitling with their edge intact.

parameters.name: Character and relationship legibility
parameters.definition: Clarity of the social topology: kinship, status, and bonds readable at speed.

1.text: Confusing - the viewer cannot map who is who or how anyone relates.

2.text: Muddled - core pair is clear but everyone else blurs; name overload.

3.text: Patchy - key relationships legible only after effort or rewatching.

4.text: Mostly readable - one relationship or status link remains unclear.

5.text: Clear enough - the social map is readable, if plain; roles arrive a beat late in places.

6.text: Clear - kinship, status, and bonds land on first viewing.

7.text: Strong - relationships are established instantly through role labels and behavior, not exposition.

8.text: Very strong - the topology is legible and does dramatic work; status shifts read in real time.

9.text: Near-effortless - instant, memorable social map with a single soft edge.

10.text: Effortless - every relationship and status line is instantly legible and productive; no viewer ever asks who someone is.

parameters.name: Series continuity and escalation
parameters.definition: How well the episode serves the season arc: callbacks, planted debt, payoff chaining.

1.text: Disconnected - the episode ignores the arc; filler with forgotten secrets.

2.text: Loose - a nominal link to the arc that changes nothing.

3.text: Shaky - continuity exists but wobbles; planted debts go unacknowledged.

4.text: Serviceable-minus - the episode advances the arc but pays off little it planted.

5.text: Adequate - respects continuity and plants or pays at least one debt.

6.text: Connected - callbacks land; the episode both pays and plants.

7.text: Integrated - the episode's function in the season is explicit; reveals chain cleanly across episodes.

8.text: Tightly integrated - escalation is cumulative; earlier plants pay at maximum value.

9.text: Near-elegant - the chaining is invisible until it strikes; one link short of perfect.

10.text: Elegantly cumulative - every callback, debt, and payoff compounds; the episode is both complete and inseparable from the arc.

parameters.name: Genre/trope fit and freshness
parameters.definition: Formula competence plus novelty: expected pleasure delivered with a distinct spin.

1.text: Wrong-fit - the episode misunderstands its genre contract; expected pleasures never arrive.

2.text: Off-genre - the contract is half-honored; tone or beats fight the format.

3.text: Derivative - competent tropes with zero angle; indistinguishable from the pile.

4.text: Standard-minus - the formula works but any spin is cosmetic.

5.text: Acceptable - delivers the genre's pleasures cleanly; freshness is modest.

6.text: Engaging - formula plus one genuine twist on setting, competence, or moral line.

7.text: Satisfying - the expected pleasure lands with a distinct, ownable spin.

8.text: Very satisfying - the spin reshapes familiar beats; the episode feels new without breaking contract.

9.text: Near-exceptional - familiar made newly energized; a notch from defining its subgenre.

10.text: Familiar but newly energized - honors the formula perfectly while making it feel invented yesterday.

parameters.name: Localization and cultural fit
parameters.definition: Credibility in the target market: idiom, ritual, institutions, and shame logic feel lived, not translated.

1.text: False - the world reads imported; idiom, ritual, and institutions ring untrue for the market.

2.text: Mostly false - isolated authentic touches inside a translated frame.

3.text: Spotty - authenticity comes and goes; key beats (custom, kinship, procedure) feel off.

4.text: Uneven - the world mostly holds but dialogue or ritual slips into translationese.

5.text: Passable - a native viewer would not object, though little feels specifically local.

6.text: Grounded - local idiom and social rules do real work in at least one beat.

7.text: Credible - conflict runs through local social rules; shame and honor logic are native.

8.text: Very credible - the market's texture shapes plot decisions, not just dressing.

9.text: Near-native - lived-in specificity throughout; one soft note from market-native.

10.text: Market-native - indistinguishable from a story conceived inside the culture; nothing reads translated.

parameters.name: Monetization, production, and platform compliance fit
parameters.definition: Ability to work on the actual platform and locations safely and affordably: paywall placement, cast/location and safety discipline.

1.text: Unusable - the script cannot be produced or published as written: unsafe content, impossible logistics, or no workable paywall structure.

2.text: Severely constrained - major compliance or production blockers with no obvious fixes.

3.text: Risky - expensive sprawl or content near compliance lines; the paywall beat is weak or misplaced.

4.text: Marginal - producible with compromises; monetization structure underexploited.

5.text: Workable - fits budget, safety, and platform norms; paywall placement functional.

6.text: Sound - disciplined logistics and a deliberate paywall beat.

7.text: Strong - production is lean by design and the monetization beat is engineered into the structure.

8.text: Very strong - cost discipline and compliance headroom with a high-conversion gate beat.

9.text: Near-optimized - production and monetization logic reinforce the drama; trivial slack remains.

10.text: Optimized - maximum drama per rupee, airtight compliance, and a paywall beat the structure was built around.

composites.name: Craft Quality
composites.formulaText: CQ = (SUM(w_i x s_i) / SUM(w_i)) x 10, using parameters 1-11 with effective (context-adjusted) weights. Excludes monetization_compliance.

composites.name: Commercial Pull
composites.formulaText: CP = 10 x (0.25 x opening_hook + 0.20 x conflict_stakes + 0.20 x cliffhanger_pull + 0.15 x genre_freshness + 0.10 x localization_fit + 0.10 x monetization_compliance). 'Scaled to 100' resolved as x10 per founder ruling 3.

composites.name: Risk
composites.formulaText: R = 100 - 10 x (0.4 x localization_fit + 0.6 x monetization_compliance); +15 if a severe safety/compliance red flag is raised (source: 'adjusted upward for any known red-flag issue', quantified as +15 in Rubric v1 - see SYNTHESIS_NOTES). Higher is worse; R is SUBTRACTED in Overall.

composites.name: Overall
composites.formulaText: Overall = 0.60 x CQ + 0.30 x CP - 0.10 x R, clamped to 0-100. The formula governs; the source's worked example (71 vs computed 66.0) is rejected per founder ruling 4.

priority.formulaText: Priority_i = effectiveWeight_i x (10 - score_i) x contextMultiplier_i; top items are the fix-first list.

gates.description: First-episode hook below 4: the opener cannot carry a series launch.
Non-finale cliffhanger below 4: an episode that does not pull forward breaks the binge chain.
Localization below 4 on a market-specific commissioning: the script will not land in its target market.

Monetization/production/compliance below 4: the script cannot be produced or published viably. Applies only when platform context was provided (missing context must lower confidence, never fail - see SYNTHESIS_NOTES).
Continuity below 4 at midpoint or finale: the arc-carrying episodes cannot ignore the arc.
Any safety/compliance red flag marked severe (obscenity/IRWA exposure, POCSO risk, drug glorification, harmful misrepresentation).

modes.note: Video path: transcript + joint AV read scored against this rubric. Base weights.
Paste-script path. Base weights; context-starved parameters carry low-confidence markers.
In config for completeness (source mode); no UI path in v1. Confidence deduction applies.
Full scripted batch (source mode); no UI path in v1.

weightShift.note: Founder ruling 5: shifts are +/-20% relative, renormalized to 100, and every shifted weight is flagged derived in the result.

gateCap.note: Founder ruling 1: every triggered gate is major severity and caps the verdict at rewrite_heavily (displayed 'Rework'); only a severe safety/compliance flag forces reject ('Not in this form'). Founder ruling 6: a triggered gate always blocks both Ready labels. Ladder is read top-down, first match (ruling 2).
